beats.narration_text: This is Liam for Humanitarians AI.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · The Book of Why: The New Science of Cause and Effect — Book Review

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Classical statistics carried an original sin: it could measure correlation but had no real language for cause and effect. Without that language, doctors couldn't separate confounders from mediators, so they prescribed ineffective treatments. Causal diagrams encode your assumptions as arrows, turning hidden guesses into claims you can actually test. Transparency enables scientific communication; opacity hides the assumptions and prevents anyone from checking them.

artifactLines: Classical statistics carried an original sin: it could measure correlation but had no real language for cause and effect.
Without that language, doctors couldn't separate confounders from mediators, so they prescribed ineffective treatments.
Causal diagrams encode your assumptions as arrows, turning hidden guesses into claims you can actually test.
Transparency enables scientific communication; opacity hides the assumptions and prevents anyone from checking them.

beats.narration_text: Here is why The Book of Why: The New Science of Cause and Effect — Book Review belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: Classical statistics carried an original sin: it could measure correlation but had no real language for cause and effect.
Without that language, doctors couldn't separate confounders from mediators, so they prescribed ineffective treatments.
Causal diagrams encode your assumptions as arrows, turning hidden guesses into claims you can actually test.
Transparency enables scientific communication; opacity hides the assumptions and prevents anyone from checking them.

beats.narration_text: I want to test the confounder-versus-mediator distinction myself, since you said doctors prescribed useless treatments when they couldn't tell the two apart. Help me: (1) sketch a causal diagram for a simple treatment-and-recovery scenario with one confounder and one mediator, (2) show which arrow I would wrongly control for if I only had statistics and no diagram, and (3) explain how drawing it makes that hidden assumption testable.
